sentences.text: Anything could happen and nothing happened.
We were fully at what they wanted to do to you.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was the psychological dread of the camp that was the worst part of it.
They could take you out in the mud and have you exercise in the mud, then afterwards come home and clean up as little or as much as possible.
At that time, we still didn't have any numbers on the arms, only on the uniforms.
The uniform was next to nothing in the middle of the winter.
We froze-- no gloves, nothing, not even hats.
Some later on they had some hats, but they give us only what they had, and then it was all used.
We didn't know anything.
That I don't remember anymore.
For example, you had on the towers there, there was always a man at the machine gun.
He could always shoot.
Didn't need much of an excuse, either.
Pardon?
Get out.
Get out while you still can.
Only if there-- your relatives or so got you a ship ticket out of Europe.
Shanghai was the only possibility.
That's why so many of the refugees went to Shanghai.
And there are very few Eastern European refugees-- they're mainly German and Austrian-- in Dachau, itself, at that time.
They called you the first thing in the morning.
Came into the cabin, called your name, and you went to a place where you stood in line, and they told you.
And then you could go home, but you had to buy your railroad tickets.
You better have the money for it, or the others had to help to get the money.
The SS wouldn't do anything.
Three and a half months, in the camp itself from November 14.
I was arrested the 10th, November 14--
Until April 21st.
Yeah, '38 to '39.
They called me and said-- we stood in line, the few that were released on that particular day.
And they took us to the depot, local depot there, and we waited for the train.
We had to buy the ticket, and then we went.
In Munich, we had to wait then for the regular train.
You didn't ask.
You were glad, because whatever they would tell you, they wouldn't tell you the truth anyhow.
A big, big stone fell off your heart.
You felt like a human being again.
And then, the next day after you came home, you had to report to the Gestapo.
The main office in Vienna of the Gestapo.
And you had to stand with your face against the wall.
Supposedly-- that was one of their tricks to frighten you.
And some of those crooks, or whatever you want to call it, they were miserable.
They called you all kinds of things.
[NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] means?
[NON-ENGLISH] is a female pig.
To call a Jew anything in connection with pig was supposed to humiliate him.
The majority were not.

They were in fear, because the teachers in school, especially high school and grammar school, the teachers ask the children what their parents talked about, especially in the lower grade grammar school.
Then they reported it to Gestapo.
In the house where we lived, there was one family, for a typical example, a widow.
She was a Nazi, and one of her daughters was a Nazi.
Another daughter was a religious Catholic.
She didn't want any part of it.
And the son was a communist, and somebody warned him that his own mother reported him to the Gestapo, so he escaped.
Some of the old spinsters, sounds ironical and funny, but it is-- they meant it.
They had a little altar, but not with Jesus, but with Hitler picture, and pray to Hitler.
You had one phenomenon culturally important-- before Hitler came to Vienna, the churches were empty except for the old spinsters.
And Hitler came.
Within a week, the churches were full.
They needed something to get away from that atmosphere.
The Austrian troops and the German officers, they rebelled.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The police had to do their duty, but they never mistreated us.
It was only the SS.
It was March 13, I think.
No March 10-- 11-- but Hitler came to Vienna on March 13.
No, just to show you the precaution they took, he went on certain main streets.
On both sides of it, there were SS troops, one facing toward the houses, the other one facing the street in a complete chain.
In the buildings, where there were Jews living, they had to sit next to the back wall, couldn't go to the window.
And there was an SS man watching.
They had 100% apparatus to suppress anything that could happen.
Pardon?
Look, it was easily done.
First of all, the SS set the tune.
And second of all, whenever you have these-- you have the mass hypnosis effect there, when they yell, hurray, you yell hurray, too.
Because you don't know what their next probably will say.
The only way you could be independent-- if you never went there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You were under complete, constant control.
No, and I was absolutely not curious.
As a Jew, you better stay out of those speeches.
In Dachau, when I was there, you didn't have any eastern Jews, so I would have to just fantasize.
Later on, actually, especially when they went into Poland, some people were in those camps.
That was mainly eastern Jews.
For Dachau, and for another few camps there. .
Also the [NON-ENGLISH].
But it was mainly-- look, we, in the camp in Dachau, we had to set up facilities for 30,000 Sudeten Germans-- not Jews-- who were arrested because they were not for Hitler.
So they wanted to make space for them there, when we would be out-- 30,000.
From then on, I just wanted to get out.
Then Father finally got the ticket for me.
From then on, I had to get ready, pack whatever I was permitted to take with me.
Then I had to go through customs inspection.
I had to get a supplement to the passport, because it was outside of Europe.
You see, the passport normally was for European travel.
Then you needed from the-- what they called it [NON-ENGLISH], that means a place where you wander to, see?
There, you had a confirmation for-- an OK from them in the passport that you could go.
And the passport had a big J in it.
But I didn't have yet the Israel.
So in Manila, when my passport was about to expire when I wanted to go to the United States-- so I asked-- I thought the best way to-- is find out first.
So I went to the American High Commissioner, who was very nice.
And I told him the passport is about to expire, and when I go to the States and Europe, what we-- what-- what should I do?
He said, you go to the German embassy there-- consulate.
They don't give it to you, don't worry.
We'll give you papers He was very nice.
So I went.
So they gave me the passport.
They said, how about putting Israel into the passport?
I said, I will do that when I want, not now.
They didn't have to say anything.
Where did you go from Vienna?
From Vienna I went to Shanghai.
With an Italian steamer, because the Italians had an agreement with the Germans.
They could take the refugees and all to Shanghai.
It was a perpetual trade.
My mother was dead already, and Father-- he knew and I would-- he knew I would try to see that he could get out, too.
But there was a Jewish woman who had an affidavit for two, because she had a daughter, but the daughter was in Belgium.
They thought it was safe at that time.
So she would take Father with her.
Cancer-- she suffered for about three, four months terribly.
Pardon?
No, that was before I went.
That was the one good thing.
It was before I went to the camp.
She died in, I think, September or so.
No, August 31, she died.
Father-- naturally, the store was closed.
He couldn't touch anything there.
It was agitating.
He had more courage than I.
He went out still just to get out in the air.
And then this lady whom-- she had no mother, and she-- look, she lost her store, too.
She had a store, too.
And I don't what his whole thing was.
Anyhow, she moved in.
Should I tell my father she can't?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- my feelings were different, by that time, after all.
Then I was able to help them to come to the States.
So, at that time, I didn't even know until the last moment whether they would be on the boat to land.
It was a [NON-ENGLISH], the Italian, big steamer-- New York.
So I took one of the buses without interruption to New York.
I traveled two or three days without stop.
I mean, we stop just for meals.
We slept on the bus.
Went to New York and arrived there in the morning.
And in the afternoon, the ship was due.
So I went to the port, and luckily they were there.
He said afterwards, till the last moment, they didn't know whether the ship would land.
It was the last foreign ship that could land there-- Italian-- because then the war had started.
So I was lucky to get them out.
So they went to Washington, because she had two brothers in Washington.
Well, we kind of skipped Shanghai there.
When you left your father in Vienna to go to Shanghai, what was your understanding with your father when you parted company?
He knew I would try.
I knew through that woman he had an affidavit.
It was a question of when the American consulate would give him the visa.
And there was enough money there so that they could exist.
And that's why I want to bring up specifically-- our landlord, who was a very fine gentleman-- Catholic.
He hid our money, so that when the Nazis came with the Gestapo into our apartment, they wouldn't find any money that they could steal.
He didn't touch a penny of it.
Yeah, Geyer-- G-E-Y-E-R.
Mr.
Geyer-- his wife later on died.
She was a wonderful person, too.
And I-- he died later after the war, but I still had one or two letters with him.
He wanted me to come to Vienna to report on this one Nazi, who was indirectly-- directly responsible.
They took me into the camp.
I said, look, leave me alone.
There are enough witnesses against him there.
They don't need me.
Why should they spend money on that?
I didn't want to have anything to do with it.
I had no intention to go back to Austria.
Well, talk about Shanghai.
How long were you there?
That was from-- that was-- let's see-- I left 6 of March out of Trieste.
It took about 28 days.
So it was March, April.
I got to May-- I got sometime in May to Shanghai.
There I was until the end of June, because then they transferred me to-- as office manager to Manila.
And in Manila, I go a visa in October, and in November I left on a freighter.
Look, the city was interesting.
And I will say one thing that will contradict much of so-called evidence-- the Japanese were not bad to us.
The one thing-- you had to show a little bit of respect, because for the Asiatic, the Chinese, as well as the Japanese, if you don't show respect, it's the worst offense for them.
They lose face.
Then they can go raving mad.
But as long As you treat them with a certain amount of politeness, they won't touch you.
That year before I went there, I was told really, sincerely they had taken about 200 Jewish children to vacations to Japan.
They would have done it then, too, but then the Nazis and Germans interfered-- they didn't permit them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the German U-boats were already all around Japan.
There was-- there were two Jewish communities, actually.
The one, [NON-ENGLISH], he tried to help the Jews, too.
He was a converted Jew.
He was a-- a Christian.
The other one, I had little to do with them, because I looked-- I ran around to find a job.
And I had a job.
I didn't have time for it.
And then, luckily, they sent me to Manila.
And in Manila, I was on my own.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There, in Manila, there was a small Jewish community.
But the Anglos, as we used to call them, didn't have anything to do with us.
We were also whites.
I was so busy with trying to do what I have to to achieve what I wanted, that I didn't have much time.
I had three or four books with me that passed the inspection of the Nazis when I left.
And I was hungry-- always hungry, because I couldn't eat the food that they had in the camp.
Only thing I ate there was maybe a piece of bread and some soup.
No, because it was distasteful.
The eggs were small eggs.
When you opened them, they smelled all over the place.
So some people ate it.
I didn't.
First of all, you had to have money to buy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was nobody to send money.
An uncle of mine sent me about $10 that time, from Czechoslovakia.
But what was $10?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] For him, it was a lot of money, but there, it was not.
Pardon?
No, from Shanghai, I went to Manila.

And I-- the one thing that was a good thing I did, too-- before leaving Shanghai, I went to the American consulate there, and I told them my new address, that all my documents should be transferred to Manila.
Otherwise, there were so many Jews in Shanghai, I probably would have had to wait for years.
But in Manila there were not many, so I got on the quota.
So they called me in on October 15, '39.
I had a physical.
The man there was exceptionally nice-- their consul-- exceptionally nice.
He said I was lucky in a way to the hard work of somebody else.
There was a man ahead of me for that particular visa, but he was sick.
He couldn't pass the exam.
So he called me that the consulate if I would be interested in taking his place.
I said, certainly.
You give me a few days time.
And then, something that may interest people-- I don't know whether you want it in it or not.
There-- there was a big rumor that the British duke in Hong Kong-- everybody with a German passport off the boat.
So I thought, there's only one way of finding out.
I went to the head commissioner-- the American head commissioner-- very nice too.
He said, we don't know of anything, but I will try to find out.
Could you come back in two weeks?
I said, anything just so that I know.
So I came back.
They said, I hate to tell you, it's right.
They take everybody with a German passport-- even with a J in it-- off the boat.
And if you are not able to land within four months in the United States, your visa is invalid.
You don't get another one.
So they advised, take one of the freighters that doesn't stop anywhere until it gets you in the United States.
So there was a Norwegian freighter, but went for a Manila shipping company-- copper boat.
They transported copper to the United States.
So, I was one of seven passengers on that boat-- so that got me over to the States.
Where did you arrive at?
I should have arrived in San Francisco, but there was a big strike on, so I got off the boat in Los Angeles.
I thought, why should I wait until we sooner or later land in San Francisco?
I got off the boat in Los Angeles.
No, I wrote to him in Vienna, and then I got-- from the [NON-ENGLISH] in Chicago, I got a cable.
I should transfer $150 through them so that Father and his wife could get the ticket.
I didn't have any $150.
But through-- let's say luck, in a way-- there was a very wealthy Jewish man.
And the people who gave me the affidavit knew him.
He said, OK.
I was willing to sell my camera-- my photo-- my stamp collection.
Find a buyer.
So the man-- I talked to him-- said, why do you want to sell it?
I said, I need the money.
I showed him the wire from the [NON-ENGLISH] in Italy.
I have to send the money, or they won't be able to leave.
He said, forget the stamps, made out a check-- $150.
So then, later on, as soon as-- I was then married, already.
As soon as Martha and I had the money, we saw him and gave it back to him.
He was a multimillionaire.
What's his name?
I don't want to mention the name.
He was a multimillionaire, very fine.
So he said to me, look, to me that $150 doesn't mean a darn thing.
You want to keep it?
I said, no.
I owed it to you-- you were generous to loan it to me.
I repay my loan and give you my thanks.
And Martha felt the same way.
She was pleased.
Well, first of all I met her through a friend of hers-- girlfriend-- who was married to another refugee-- one from Poland.
And when I saw her-- I wouldn't say it was love at first sight, but there was something about her that attracted me-- not only the exterior, but the personality.
We started going together-- going together, because I didn't want to bind her until I had at least a job at a-- I mean, for any length of time.
So my first job, then we decided to get married.
Her parents were wonderful to me.
They were not like in-laws, they were like parents.
I couldn't have found better ones.
Only, unhappily, her father died about three years after we got married.
Her mother lived much longer.
She was a very wonderful person.
Chicago.
Father, yeah, and his wife-- they came to New York.
That's when I took the bus to New York to make sure-- around to the last moment, I didn't know.
They got on.
They told me that she had two brothers in Washington.
So they wanted to go to Washington.
And she said to me, now, you move everything and come to Washington.
I said, sorry.
I'm engaged in Chicago.
That comes first.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She didn't like that idea at all, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was better that way.
Look, first of all, when we got married-- I'd-- you see, with her, I could discuss everything and anything.
There was no secrets.
So I asked her, look, Martha, do you think you want to have children?
We can today if you want to.
She said, why do you ask?

I said, to be very frank, if you don't want, after my experience with Dachau and Nazis, I wouldn't want any children of ours to be exposed to it, because that is something that somehow or other will come back.
She said, we won't have any children.
She agreed with me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what goes on today in the world, I'm twice as glad.
She would have been a wonderful mother.
Something similar to what happened to me.
Pardon?
Even in the United States.
Maybe not exactly the same, but similar.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Don't forget the Ku Klux Klan was still much stronger than it is now.
Not the fear-- it was I didn't want to feel that any children or grandchildren of ours should be exposed to those dangers.
It wasn't a fear.
It was I just couldn't see it.
Yeah.
Pardon?
At that time I worked for Wards-- in the small departments.
I supervised there.
I was about the only Jew there in the retail division.
Then when the war progressed, I-- I was well known there and liked there.
They knew that I was Jewish.
I never made a secret of it.
So I talked to my higher superior-- the system controller.
I said to him, look, you tell me that I'm one of your right-hand boys.
What is my possibilities with you?
He said, I love you, I like you, I have to be very frank with you.
As a Jew, you cannot go much further.
Your people like you.
They work with you, but I don't know what the others will do.
So that's when I was-- had the possibility to move to California, and to have a defense job as a accountant-- a cost accountant.
Martha moved too.
She gave up a very good job there, because there was a jealousy between California and Illinois.
She had been a senior chief interviewer with the United States Employment Service in the main office in Chicago.
But here, they didn't want to give her credit for it, just a much lower-- so we said, forget it.
She got a job also with the consolidated-- it was a different department-- as security to the assistant director of transportation.
So when we saw then towards the end, in '45, that it was going to end, we were about 40,000 employees.
What would they do with it?
So Martha and I talked it over.
I first thought we should move to San Francisco.
So she said, look, do you mind very much if we go to Chicago?
Mother is not very well.
Do you want to go to Chicago?
We go to Chicago.
So we went to Chicago.
So we started all over again.
